# Methods

## Meta-stochastic frontier analysis

Technological adoption in Ghana's agriculture sector is shaped by a variety of factors, including geography, gender, and access to extension services [@ref47; @ref48; @ref49; @ref50; @ref51; @ref52]. However, the role of land ownership in influencing technology use and agricultural performance has received comparatively limited attention. Farmland ownership can affect productivity and efficiency in multiple ways. First, ownership often improves access to credit and long-term investments, encouraging adoption of productivity-enhancing technologies [@ref53; @ref54]. Second, insecure land rights may discourage such investments due to fear of expropriation or disputes [@ref55; @ref56]. Third, ownership may increase access to support services, information, and extension, which further facilitates improved farming practices [@ref50; @ref51].

Given these considerations, it is essential to account for technological heterogeneity when assessing farmers' performance relative to the production frontier. Ignoring these structural differences in land tenure could result in biased measures of production inefficiency. To address this, we categorize farmers into two groups — landowners and non-landowners — and apply a two-step Meta-Stochastic Frontier (MSF) approach. This technique enables us to compare efficiency within groups that operate under different land tenure regimes and to capture the productivity gap linked to ownership.

We assume that farmers in each group operate under a common technology but may differ in their ability to utilize it effectively. Within each group, we estimate a Stochastic Frontier (SF) model, where deviations from the frontier may result from technical inefficiency or random shocks. Importantly, a farmer's performance may also exceed the group-specific frontier due to favourable production shocks.

Following earlier studies that have modelled agricultural production in Ghana using both Cobb-Douglas and Translog specifications [@ref50; @ref57; @ref58], we adopt the Translog production function due to its greater flexibility. A formal likelihood ratio test rejects the Cobb-Douglas form at the 1% level. However, preliminary Translog estimates raised theoretical concerns, such as violations of monotonicity and quasi-concavity. To address this, we apply a standard three-step correction procedure, imposing monotonicity and evaluating quasi-concavity post-estimation. After correction, monotonicity is satisfied for 71% to 99.9% of observations and curvature for 20% to 58%, depending on the frontier (Table 3).

Our group-specific stochastic production frontier for farmers in group $j$ is specified as:

$f^{j}\left( x_{\text{ijt}} \right) = \ln y_{\text{ijt}} = \beta_{0j} + \sum_{k}^{}{\beta_{\text{kj}}\ln x_{\text{kijt}}} + \frac{1}{2}\sum_{s}^{}{\sum_{k}^{}{\beta_{\text{skj}}\ln x_{\text{kijt}}\ln x_{\text{sijt}}}} + \beta_{h}h_{\text{ijt}} + v_{\text{ijt}} - u_{\text{ijt}}$

$u_{\text{ijt}}\sim N^{+}\left\lbrack 0,exp\left( w_{\text{ijt}}\alpha \right) \right\rbrack$, $v_{\text{ijt}}\sim N\left( 0,\sigma_{\text{vj}}^{2} \right)$ (1)

where $y_{\text{ijt}}$ is the total value of crop output for farmer $i$ in group $j$ in time (survey) *t*; $\text{\ x}_{\text{kijt}}$ is input $k$ of farmer $i$, representing inputs such as land, planting materials, family labour, hired labour, fertilizer, and pesticide. The vector $h_{\text{ijt}}$ includes production shifters such as agroecological zone and crop composition.

We assume a half-normal distribution for the inefficiency term due to convergence issues with more flexible distributions in the subgroup analyses. The vector $w_{\text{ijt}}$ includes farmer characteristics (age, gender, education), institutional variables (land tenure, access to credit, mechanization, extension), crop diversification, and zone fixed effects. This structure aligns with prior empirical work in the field [@ref48; @ref50; @ref57; @ref58].

The technical efficiency (TE) of a farmer relative to their group-specific frontier is computed as: $\text{TE}_{\text{ijt}} = \ E\left\lbrack \exp\left( - u_{\text{ijt}} \right)|{\widehat{\varepsilon}}_{\text{ijt}} \right\rbrack$ (2)

In the second stage of the MSF, we pool the predicted outputs from both landownership groups and estimate a meta-frontier function that envelops the group-specific frontiers:

$f^{M}\left( x_{\text{ijt}} \right) = \ln{\widehat{y}}_{\text{ijt}} = \beta_{0M} + \sum_{k}^{}{\beta_{\text{kM}}\ln x_{\text{kiMt}}} + \frac{1}{2}\sum_{s}^{}{\sum_{k}^{}{\beta_{\text{skM}}\ln x_{\text{kiMt}}\ln x_{\text{siMt}}}} + \beta_{h}h_{\text{ijt}} - u_{\text{Mijt}}$ (3)

The meta-frontier inefficiency term $u_{\text{iM}}\sim N^{+}\left( 0,\exp\left( w_{i}\alpha_{M} \right) \right) > 0\ $captures the technology gap between a group's frontier and the meta-frontier.

This allows us to compute the Technology Gap Ratio (TGR)

$\text{TGR}_{\text{ijt}} = \frac{f^{j}\left( x_{\text{ijt}} \right)}{f^{M}\left( x_{\text{ijt}} \right)} = e^{- u_{\text{iM}}} \leq 1$ (4)

TGR reflects how close each group's technology is to the best available (meta) technology. Combined with TE, it allows us to calculate Meta-Technical Efficiency (MTE), which measures each farmer's performance relative to the meta-frontier.

$\text{MTE}_{\text{ijt}} = \ f^{j}\left( x_{\text{ijt}} \right)\left\lbrack f^{M}\left( x_{\text{ijt}} \right)e^{v_{\text{ijt}}} \right\rbrack^{- 1} = \text{TGR}_{\text{ijt}} \times \text{TE}_{\text{ijt}}$ (5)

We estimate all models using maximum likelihood estimation, derive input elasticities from the first-order partial derivatives of the estimated frontier, and calculate returns to scale (RTS) by summing elasticities. This framework allows us to assess not only technical efficiency but also how differences in land tenure influence access to and use of productive technologies.

## Identification

Classification of farmers into landowners and non-landowners is based on self-reported land ownership status, which may be subject to measurement errors due to variation in farmers\' interpretation of ownership, tenure arrangements, and local customary norms. To reduce such errors and minimize model dependency, we adopt a matched sample approach in estimating the meta-stochastic frontier (Equation 3), consistent with established practices in the literature [@ref59; @ref60]. While our approach borrows from classical matching techniques [@ref61; @ref65] it differs in that we do not use matching to impute missing values. Rather, we use it to create a balanced dataset that enables more reliable estimation of group-specific frontiers by comparing farmers with and without land ownership under similar observable conditions.

In our case, matching is based on a wide range of farmer, household, and production characteristics. These include age, education, marital status, religion, ethnicity, relationship to the household head, household size, dependency ratio, female share, crop land allocation, and land tenure status. Crucially, matching is restricted within the same gender, GLSS wave, region, agroecological zone, and rural/urban locality, to ensure that observed differences in technical efficiency are attributable primarily to differences in land ownership rather than other confounding contextual factors.

While matching on a single covariate is straightforward, balancing treated (landowners) and untreated (non-landowners) groups across multiple continuous and categorical variables introduces complexity. Therefore, we implement one-to-one nearest-neighbor matching, drawing on several distance metrics, including Euclidean distance, scaled Euclidean, propensity scores (using Logit, Probit, Complementary log-log, and Cauchit link functions), Mahalanobis [@ref62], and robust Mahalanobis distances [@ref63]. As shown in Figure S1, we evaluate the effectiveness of each matching method using standardized mean differences and variance ratios, with optimal balance indicated by values close to zero and one, respectively [@ref64; @ref65]. Among these approaches, robust Mahalanobis distance matching achieved the best covariate balance and is used in the main analysis.

Given the differences in sampling strategies, question structure, and implementation across the GLSS rounds, treating the pooled data as a single cross-section would risk masking important variation. To account for this, we employ a two-step estimation strategy. In the first step, the MSF model (Equations 1--5) is estimated separately for each GLSS wave, allowing for flexibility in production technologies and inefficiency structures across time. Efficiency scores and input elasticities including Technical Efficiency (TE), Technology Gap Ratio (TGR), and Meta-Technical Efficiency (MTE) are then computed within each wave. In the second step, these values are averaged across surveys, ensuring comparability while accounting for survey-specific heterogeneity.

To calculate robust standard errors, we apply a jackknife resampling technique. For each survey wave, we create 100 resampled datasets by systematically excluding one Enumeration Area (EA) per iteration and re-estimating the full procedure, including the stochastic frontier, matching, and meta-frontier estimations. This resampling framework accounts for the hierarchical survey design and accommodates the fact that the procedure used to enforce theoretical properties (monotonicity and quasi-concavity) in Equations (1) and (3) prevents estimation of a conventional variance-covariance matrix. Each jackknife iteration involves running the full estimation process within each wave, ensuring internal consistency across all stages of the analysis.
